title: Why Maternal Vaccines Are Important | Pregnancy & Vaccines | CDC
text:

There is also an increased risk of complications that can affect a patient’s pregnancy and baby including, preterm birth or stillbirth. COVID-19 vaccination remains the best protection against COVID-19-related hospitalization and death for both your patient and their baby.
For Everyone 
COVID-19 
Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) 
Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is recognized as one of the most common causes of childhood illness and is the most common cause of hospitalization in infants. It causes annual outbreaks of respiratory illnesses in all age groups. In most regions of the United States, RSV season starts in the fall and peaks in the winter, but the timing and severity of RSV season in a given community can vary from year to year. Generally, there are two options to protect infants from RSV: either maternal RSV vaccination or infant immunization with nirsevimab (RSV monoclonal antibody). RSV vaccination is recommended for pregnant women at 32 through 36 weeks gestation, and it is administered seasonally from September through January in most of the continental United States*.
